segments.s158-g1: چنین گفت موبد که is a reporting clause: چنين (thus) modifies the speech verb گفت (past tense 'said'); موبد is the agent (subject) following the verb — a poetic inversion. که introduces the content clause; یک‌روز طوس is a circumstantial time phrase plus the subject (Tūs) of the subordinate clause (literal order: 'one day Tūs').
segments.s158-g2: بدانگه is an adverbial/place-time phrase 'at that place/thereupon' tying to the preceding clause; که here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when/that' introducing the subordinate clause. برخاست is a past-tense intransitive verb 'arose/rose', and بانگ خروس is the verbal subject (verb precedes its noun in this inverted, poetic order) meaning 'the cock's cry'.
segments.s158-g3: This is a coordinated subject list: خود ('himself' / emphatic 'he himself') precedes the names joined by و. چندی is a quantifier 'several/some' modifying سوار ('riders/horsemen'), so 'and some horsemen' completes the enumerated group.
segments.s158-g4: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went off/rode away' whose subject is the preceding coordinated group. شاد functions adverbially here ('gladly/joyful'). از در شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'from the king's gate' in which از marks origin, در means 'door/gate' (not the preposition), and شهریار is the possessor.
segments.s158-g5: Two destination/goal phrases introduced by به: به نخچیر گوران = 'to/for the Guran's prey' (نخچیر = 'game, prey'; گوران is the tribal name), and به دشت دغوی = 'to the clamorous/plain that is noisy' (دشت = plain; دغوی = noisy/clamorous), both marking where the hunting takes place.
segments.s158-g6: A nominal list of hunting animals: ابا باز and یوزان joined by و, followed by the attributive نخچیرجوی ('seeker of prey'). Grammatically, نخچیرجوی modifies the preceding nouns. Note: ابا here appears as part of the compound name for a hawk/falconer type and does not match the provided dictionary senses exactly.
segments.s158-g7: فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'abundantly/plentifully' modifying the coordinated verbs. گرفتند و انداختند are past-tense plural verbs 'they took/ seized and they placed/laid (down)'; the two verbs form a compound action (collecting and storing).
segments.s158-g8: علوفه ('forage') is the direct object, followed by the postnominal adjective چهل روزه ('forty-day' i.e. 'for forty days'), then the object marker را and the verb ساختند ('they made/prepared') in past plural. Word order (object + adjective + را + verb) is normal in Persian for a constructed object phrase followed by its verb.
segments.s158-g89: This is a compact existential clause. دگر (literally “other/remaining”) prefaces the noun phrase and narrows it to “the other” or “whatever remains.” ایزدی is an adjective meaning “divine/godlike” modifying the implied noun(s). هرچه بایست is a relative/indefinite expression meaning “whatever ought to be” where بایست (an older/polite subjunctive/obligative form) carries the sense of “should/ought to be.” The finite verb بود (past of بودن) follows to assert existence: “there was.”
segments.s158-g90: یکی سرخ یاقوت is a simple noun phrase: یکی (the numeral/indefinite “one/a single”) followed by the adjective سرخ “red” and the noun یاقوت “ruby.” The final two words, بد نابسود, form an older/poetic adjectival qualifier: نابسود literally means “fruitless/to no avail,” and here the preceding بد is used idiomatically (not in its plain sense “bad”) to modify that adjective — together functioning as a fixed poetic collocation meaning something like “not to be wasted / not fruitless / of enduring value.” In short, the whole segment names a single red ruby and qualifies it with a traditional poetic phrase indicating it is not worthless or wasted.
segments.s158-g65: چو here is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'. The clause is SOV: subject (کاووس) + object phrase (رویِ کنیزک, with روی 'face' governing the possessive/adjunct کنیزک) followed by the past verb بدید 'saw'. This is a standard narrative past construction in classical Persian.
segments.s158-g66: بخندید is the past (3rd person) narrative 'he laughed'. The coordinating و links to the second past clause لب را به دندان گزید where را marks لب as a definite/direct object and به دندان is an instrumental phrase 'with (his) teeth'; گزید here is the past 'bit/stung' (not 'choose'). Together the two verbs describe successive actions by the same subject.
segments.s158-g67: بهردو سپهبد is an indirect object phrase 'to both spahbeds' (به + هردو). Such datives commonly precede the verb in poetry. چنین گفت شاه inverts the expected order and places the adverb 'thus' before the past verb گفت; شاه is the nominative subject placed after the verb for emphasis (verb–subject order is common in older Persian).
segments.s158-g68: که here introduces reported speech ('that' / introducing the king's words). کوتاه شد is a compound past/passive sense 'was shortened' or 'became short' (شد = became). بر شما is a dative/benefactive phrase 'for you', and رنج راه is a noun phrase 'the hardship of the road' (رنج + راه). The whole segment is the content of the quoted utterance.
segments.s158-g69: برین داستان is a prepositional phrase 'on/for this tale' where بر = 'on' and ین is the poetic/orthographic form of 'این' ('this'). بگذرانیم is 1st plural subjunctive/optative 'let us spend' or 'we will spend' and is followed by روز 'the day' as the time-span being spent; word order is inverted for poetic effect (object/day at end).
segments.s158-g70: که again introduces a subordinate temporal clause 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. گیرند is a classical/plural-form verb here meaning 'take/seize' (used poetically; agreement is not strictly modern), and گردان بیوز is a noun phrase modifying what the sun 'takes' — گردان can be 'troop' or 'revolving' and بیوز is an adjective 'windless/weightless'; the phrase is somewhat compressed and poetic, so the exact syntactic function is determined by context (a temporal/imagistic clause modifying روز).
segments.s158-g71: گوزنست is an old-style nominative+copula construction meaning 'it is a stag' (گوزن + است fused). اگر here functions rhetorically as an alternative marker roughly 'or' (classical usage), introducing the contrasting nominal phrase آهوی دلبرست 'a gazelle, beloved'. دلبرست is copulative ('is beloved'/'is a beauty') used as a predicate describing the second option.
segments.s158-g72: شکاری چنین is a nominal phrase 'such a quarry/game' (شکاری used adjectivally/nominatively 'hunting/game-like' → 'quarry' + چنین 'such'). ازدر is a prepositional expression 'from the threshold/presence' (commonly meaning 'at the doorway' or 'before the presence of'), and مهترست is copular 'belongs to/ is fit for the chief/elder' (مهتر = chief). The clause praises the prize as fitting for the chief at the door; word order here is compressed and elevated in tone.
segments.s158-g33: پیاده is a noun 'the footman' (an attendant on foot) in subject position. بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (بد + -و enclitic), placed before the verb. گفت is the past tense 3rd-person singular 'said'. چون آمدی is an embedded interrogative clause: چون functions here as 'how' and آمدی is 2nd-person singular past 'you came'.
segments.s158-g34: که is a complementizer introducing the content of the question ('that' / 'namely'). بی‌باره is an adjective 'unladen, without baggage' (poetic 'without X'), و is the coordinating 'and', رهنمون is a noun 'guide/guidance' and آمدی repeats the 2nd-person past 'you came' — the phrase questions how he arrived both without baggage and without guidance, with a parallel (elliptical) structure.
segments.s158-g35: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the speech, داد is the past verb 'gave' and پاسخ is the noun 'answer' — together 'gave such an answer that:'. After the colon, اسپم is the possessed noun 'my steed' (اسپ + -م enclitic) and بماند is a past/resultive form 'remained / was left' (classical/poetic use of ب- prefix with مانْد stem).
segments.s158-g36: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of / from', سستی is 'weakness', مرا is the object pronoun 'me' (مـ- enclitic + را omitted or embedded), بر زمین is the adverbial 'onto the ground' (بر + زمین), and برنشاند is a past transitive verb 'caused/put/forced down' — here 'it (the horse) threw/forced me to the ground'. Word order places the reason first, then the object, then the goal, then the verb.
segments.s158-g37: بی‌اندازه is an adverb/adjective 'immeasurable, excessive' qualifying the following nouns. زر و گهر are direct objects 'gold and jewels', and داشتم is the 1st-person past 'I had'. The sequence follows the typical Persian order: (adverb) + (objects) + verb.
segments.s158-g38: به سر 'on the head' is a locative prepositional phrase; بر here reinforces location 'upon'. یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one/a single' modifying تاج 'crown', زر qualifies تاج as 'golden'. داشتم repeats the 1st-person past 'I had' (so: 'upon my head I had a single gold crown'). The doubled locatives (به ... بر) are a poetic/archaic emphasis of position.
segments.s158-g39: بران is an adverbial/particle 'thereupon / upon that' linking to the previous clause. روی بالا is a compound meaning 'the high brow/upper part (of the head)' and ز من is the prepositional phrase 'from me'. بستدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they tied/they took/they fastened' (in context classical بستادن can mean 'to remove/take off' or 'to bind' depending on object), so the clause says they removed or took that high thing from the speaker. The subject 'they' is encoded in the verb ending.
segments.s158-g40: نیام is 'scabbard' (here adverbially or as the source of the weapon), یکی 'one/a certain', تیغ 'sword', بر is the preposition 'against/on', من 'me' as object, and زدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they struck/they smote'. The phrase describes someone drawing a sword (from the scabbard) and striking the narrator.
segments.s158-g25: شب تیره مست آمد is a compact noun phrase + verb: شب (noun) + تیره (adjective after the noun, a normal poetic order) + مست (an adjectival/figurative 'intoxicated' modifying شب) followed by آمد (simple past 'came'). از دشت سور is a prepositional phrase 'from the plain (دشت) of Sur (سور)' placed after the verb as a source/locative. The whole segment describes a night arriving from a place in a poetic, personified way.
segments.s158-g26: همان چون is a temporal/discourse phrase meaning 'just then / as soon as'; مرا is the object 'me' (here presented in the text as the clitic parts 'م' + 'را'), and دید is perfect/past 'saw' (3rd person). جوشان is an adjectival/present-participial description 'seething/boiling' that characterizes 'me' (object) and z دور ('ز دور') is a prepositional phrase 'from afar'. Word order places the object (مرا) before the verb + its adjectival descriptor (جوشان) for poetic emphasis.
segments.s158-g27: یکی is an indefinite subject 'a certain / someone'; خنجری آبگون is a noun (خنجری, 'a dagger') followed by the adjective آبگون ('bluish') in the usual noun–adjective order used in poetry; برکشید is past tense 'drew up / drew (a weapon)'. The clause is a simple past action describing the drawing of the dagger.
segments.s158-g28: همان is a demonstrative 'that same (one)'; خواست is past 'desired / tried' (here 'wished to'); the prepositional phrase از تن 'from the body' precedes the object marked with را (سرم را 'my head' + object marker). برید is the past 'cut'. The order 'از تن سرم را برید' is topical/fronting of the source ('from the body') before the object 'my head' for rhythmic/poetic effect.
segments.s158-g29: بپرسید is past plural 'they asked' (classical narrative past). زو is the fused poetic form of 'از او' meaning 'of him/about him' (here listed as separate items 'ز' + 'و'); پهلوان is a vocative/appositive 'O hero' or 'the champion'; از نژاد is the prepositional phrase 'of (what) lineage'. Together the clause reports an inquiry directed to the man about his lineage.
segments.s158-g30: یک به یک کرد یاد is an idiom 'he remembered/recounted one by one' where یک به یک = 'one by one' (به functions adverbially) and کرد یاد = 'made remembrance' = 'recounted/recited'. سروبن (sarbun / سروبن) ('cypress-like ones') is the noun being enumerated; برو here functions as a demonstrative/introductory particle (poetic), linking to those cypress-like men. The verb کرد is past 'did/made' and یاد is the noun 'remembrance/recital' that together mean 'recalled/recounted.'
segments.s158-g31: بدو گفت is 'to him he said' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', گفت = 'said' in narrative past). من خویش is emphatic 'I myself' (خویش is reflexive/emphatic here), and گرسیوزم is the name Garsivaz with the 1st‑person suffix -م ('I am Garsivaz'). The clause is direct speech introduced by the narrative verb.
segments.s158-g32: به شاه آفریدون is a dative phrase 'to King Afrīdūn' (به + title + name). کشد is an archaic/poetic present or gnomic form of the verb (here glossed 'brings' / 'offers'); پروزم is پروز + ـم ('my prowes/victory/fame') and is the object: 'my victory/glory.' The clause states the speaker's claim of serving or bringing his glory to the king.
segments.s158-g81: به + noun phrase marks manner/instrument here ('with' or 'in'), followed by two adjectives (مشکوی 'musk‑scented' and زرین 'golden') and the 1st person present/subjunctive/potential verb کنم 'I do / I will do'. شایَد (شایدت) is a modal 'perhaps / may it be', carrying a possibility reading attached to the clause; the enclitic -ت on شاید in classical usage can give a sense directed at the addressee but here functions mainly as the modal particle 'perhaps'. Word order is instrument/manner + adjectives + verb + modal.
segments.s158-g82: سر 'head/top/chief' is used metaphorically as 'leader/first' and combines with the plural descriptive ماه‌رویان 'moon‑faced (beauties)'; کنَم is again the 1st person verb 'I will/make'. بایدت here expresses obligation/assurance toward the addressee ('I shall/must (do) for you' or 'it shall be so for you') and closes the promise. The noun + epithet precedes the verb in this classical word order.
segments.s158-g83: چنین داد پاسخ is literal 'thus gave an answer' (چنین 'thus', داد 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'); that clause is followed by the conjunction که introducing reported speech. Inside the quotation, دیدم is the past tense 'I saw' and ت + را together form the object 'you' (ت enclitic + را object marker yielding 'ترا').
segments.s158-g84: ز is the poetic preposition 'from', گردنکشان is a collective/epithet ('neck‑cutters' i.e. rebels/proud ones), and برگزیدم is the 1st person past 'I chose'. Again ت + را mark the 2nd‑person object 'you' (ترا). The clause means 'I chose you from among the گردنکشان.'
segments.s158-g85: بت here is the poetic/figurative 'beloved' (not literal idol), اندر شبستان places her 'into/within the bedchamber' (اندر = in/within, شبستان = inner/bed chamber), and فرستاد شاه is subject‑final classical order 'sent the king' — i.e. 'the king sent (her)'. The verb فرستاد is past tense 'sent'.
segments.s158-g86: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he commanded/said', تا introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that'), and برنشیند is the present/subjunctive form 'to sit / be seated'. به گاه is a prepositional time phrase 'at the time' (به = at, گاه = time). Together: 'he commanded that she be seated at the time.'
segments.s158-g87: بیاراستندش is the compound past verb 'they adorned / they arrayed her' (the clitic -ش marks the object). به + دیبای زرد indicates the material/garment used ('in/with yellow brocade'); classical order places the verb before the specifying materials.
segments.s158-g88: This is a simple coordinate list introduced by به 'with/in' naming the jewels: یاقوت (ruby), پیروزه (turquoise), لاجورد (lapis lazuli), connected by و 'and'. The preposition applies to the whole series, indicating they were adorned with these gems.
segments.s158-g73: بدو گفت خسرو: — literal order is 'to-her said Khosrow'. بدو is an object/indirect object pronoun 'to her' placed before the verb (typical in classical/poetic Persian). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used parenthetically to introduce direct speech). خسرو follows as the speaker's name; punctuation (:) introduces the quoted question. نژاد تو چیست — the clause is a nominative question: نژاد 'lineage' is the subject, تو 'you' is the possessor (postnominal), چی‌ست is the copula plus question 'what is'. The copula ست is attached as a clitic 'is'.
segments.s158-g74: که چهرت همانند چهر پریست؟ — که introduces the explanatory/subordinate content of the question ('that'/'how come'), linking the previous question to the following description. چهرت is the possessive form 'your face' (چهر + ـت). همانند is a preposition/adverb meaning 'like' or 'resembling' and takes the following noun. چهر پریست is a nominal comparison: چهر 'face' + پریست (here a predicative compound meaning 'fairy-like' or 'of/like a pari'). The final -ست is the copula 'is' (poetic/standard).
segments.s158-g75: ورا گفت: از مام خاتونیم — ورا (to him/her) is the indirect-object pronoun 'to him' (here 'she said to him'); گفت is 'said' (introducing her reply). از introduces origin/possession 'from/of'. مام خاتونیم is a possessive/affiliative phrase: مام خاتون (a name or title 'Mam‑Khatun' / 'mother-lady') plus the first-person copulative/possessive suffix ـیم indicating 'I am of / I belong to'. In poetic Persian names and titles often appear fused with possessive endings.
segments.s158-g76: ز سوی پدر آفریدونیم — ز (poetic variant of از) again marks 'from'/'on the side of'. سوی here means 'side' (ز سوی پدر = 'on my father's side' or 'from my father's side/lineage'). پدر is 'father' (literal or ancestral). آفریدونیم contains the name آفریدون (Afrīdūn) with the same first-person suffix ـیم: 'I am (of) Afrīdūn' (i.e. I descend from Afrīdūn). The entire segment gives paternal lineage.
segments.s158-g77: نیایم سپهدار گرسیوزست — نیایم is 'my ancestor' (نیا + ـم), serving as subject. سپهدار is a title 'commander' placed after the subject as a predicate noun. گرسیوزست is the name Garsivaz combined with the copula ـست ('is'): here the whole predicate identifies the ancestor as 'Sepahdār Garsivaz'. Word order (subject — predicate title+name — copula) is common in classical Persian.
segments.s158-g78: بران مرز خرگاه او مرکزست — بران ('upon that' / 'on that') points back to a previously mentioned border; مرز 'border' is its noun. خرگاه او is 'his khargāh' (خرگاه literally 'donkey‑stall' but used idiomatically for a humble dwelling); the genitive relation is marked by juxtaposition (خرگاه + او 'his'). مرکزست (مرکز + ست) is the predicate 'is the center'. Together: 'On that border, his humble dwelling is the center.'
segments.s158-g79: بدو گفت کاین روی و موی و نژاد — بدو 'to her' (indirect object) + گفت 'said'. کاین is a fused demonstrative/relative 'that/this' introducing the list that follows. روی و موی و نژاد lists three nouns in head-to-tail fashion: 'face and hair and lineage' (روی = face, موی = hair, نژاد = lineage). The conjunction و is simple coordination 'and'.
segments.s158-g80: همی‌خواستی داد هرسه به باد — همی‌خواستی is an archaic/poetic imperfect/conditional form meaning 'if you wished/were to wish' or 'if you desire(d)'; it introduces the hypothetical. داد is the past tense 'gave' (but here functions in a modal/optative sense, commonly rendered as 'would give' in English). هرسه 'all three' is the object; به باد 'to the wind' is an idiom meaning 'to throw away' or 'to ruin/lose' (باد is literal 'wind'). The overall sense is conditional: if you wish for these, he will cast all three to the wind.
segments.s158-g41: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' (classical usage). هشیار گردن(=گردد) is a light-verb construction meaning 'become awake/alert' (گردد = 'becomes'). پدر is the subject 'my/the father' and بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' modifying the whole clause.
segments.s158-g42: سواری فرستد is the main clause following the temporal clause; فرستد here is a third-person singular classical present/prefactual used to indicate a future action 'sends/will send' and سواری = 'a rider, horseman'. پس introduces the following short verbless clause 'پس من دمان' where the verb is omitted (typical in epic style) — دمان 'hem/skirt' with من 'I' yields an elliptical meaning 'I will cling to / take hold of his skirt' or 'I will be at his side.'
segments.s158-g43: بیاید here has an optative/indicative sense 'may come / even if they come' and together with همی gives a concessive/ emphatic tone ('even if indeed the Arabs come'). تازیان is the plural 'Arabs'. مادر + enclitic م (written here as separate token م) forms 'my mother' (possessive clitic attached to مادر).
segments.s158-g44: مادرم نخواهد introduces a subordinate clause of volition/permission: نخواهد is 'does not want / will not (allow)'. کزین = 'from this / out of this' introduces the clause 'that I...'. بوم و بر is a fixed pair meaning 'land and region/homeland', and بگذرم is first-person singular present/subjunctive 'that I pass/leave' used after نخواهد to express what she will not permit.
segments.s158-g45: دل پهلوانان is a possessed noun phrase 'the heart(s) of the champions' (possessor follows possessed). بدو is the dative 'toward him'. نرم گشت is a two-word predicate 'became soft/gentle' where گشت is the past of 'become/turn' and follows the adjective.
segments.s158-g46: سر طوس is 'the head/chief of Tūs' (the genitive link is implied), with نوذر in apposition as the name. بی‌آزرم is an adjective 'without shame' = 'shameless' (بی- prefix). گشت again is 'became' (past). The phrase thus states that the named leader turned shameless.
segments.s158-g47: شه نوذری is a titular/name phrase 'the Nowzari king' (نوذری here marks the king's affiliation or epithet). گفت: introduces direct speech. من یافتم is a simple past clause 'I found' (یافتم = first-person past of 'to find'); the object is left implicit and picked up from context.
segments.s158-g48: از ایرا = 'from Iran' where ایرا is an archaic/poetic form referring to Iran or that region. چنین تیز is an adverb + adjective sequence 'so / thus swift', and بشتافتم is the first-person past 'I hastened/rushed' (completed action), putting the manner before the verb as is normal in Persian.
segments.s158-g9: بدان جایگه is a locative phrase (ب + آن = 'to/at that' + جایگه 'place') giving the location; ترک is the subject 'a Turk'; نزدیک بود is a predicate composed of the adjective نزدیک 'near' plus the past copula بود 'was' — word order is locative → subject → predicate, typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s158-g10: زمینش is زمین 'ground/land' with the third-person possessive enclitic -ش 'his'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' linking to خرگاه 'donkey‑stall'; تاریک بود is the adjectival predicate 'was dark' (تاریک + past copula بود). The phrase states cause/relation: his ground was dark because/from the donkey‑stall.
segments.s158-g11: یکی 'a certain one / someone' is the indefinite subject; بیشه پیش اندر آمد is a compound verbal phrase: بیشه (grove) + پیش اندر آمد ('came into the front/appeared forward') where اندر آمد is the past 'came in/entered' and پیش functions as 'before/in presence of' (poetic order); ز دور is an adverbial 'from afar' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s158-g12: به نزدیک is the prepositional phrase 'toward/near to' (به + نزدیک), مرز is the object 'border/frontier', and سواران تور is the noun phrase 'the horsemen of Tūr' (سواران plural 'riders' plus the proper name تور). There is no finite verb here because this is a continuation of the previous clause.
segments.s158-g13: همی‌راند is an archaic/poetic imperfective-past form (همی- + راند) expressing continuous or habitual past action 'was riding/rode on'; در پیش 'in front/ahead' is an adverbial phrase; با طوس 'with Tous' is an accompaniment phrase. گیو is a following proper name (Giv) introduced as a companion/participant — the clause is succinct, typical of epic style.
segments.s158-g14: پس 'then' introduces the next action; اندر 'in/within' is locative; پرستنده‌ای چند is a noun phrase with the indefinite suffix -ی ('a devotee/follower') plus the quantifier چند 'a few/several' (poetic word order puts the quantifier after the noun); نیو (ney) names the reed‑flute, here attributive indicating these attendants had/played neys — the verb (played/led) is implied by context.
segments.s158-g15: بران is a locative/adverb meaning 'to/onto that' (poetic form), بیشه 'grove' is the place, رفتند is the past plural verb 'they went'; هر دو سوار is the subject 'both riders/horsemen' with the distributive هر دو 'both' placed before سوار. Note the verb precedes the explicit subject here, which is common in poetic Persian.
segments.s158-g16: بگشتند is the past plural 'they returned/turned'; بر گرد is a prepositional combination meaning 'around/about' (بر + گرد); آن مرغزار is the demonstrative + noun 'that meadow' as the object of the prepositional phrase. The clause means they circled/returned about that meadow.
segments.s158-g17: به بیشه (prepositional phrase) is fronted and means 'into/toward the grove'; یکی خوب رخ is an NP where یکی ('a certain/one') modifies خوب رخ ('beautiful-faced one'); یافتند is past-tense, 3rd-person plural 'they found' and agrees with the understood agents (the riders). Word order (prep phrase + indefinite NP + verb) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s158-g18: پر از خنده لب is an adjectival/nominal phrase: پر از = 'full of', خنده لب = 'laughter/smile of the lip' (i.e. 'smiling lips' or 'a smile on the lips'). هردو is the subject 'both', and بشتافتند is the past, 3rd-person plural 'they hurried' (moved quickly). The two parts are linked as a descriptive clause followed by the main action.
segments.s158-g19: به دیدار او is a purpose/oblique phrase 'for meeting him' (به = 'for/to' + دیدار = 'meeting', او = 'him/her'). در زمانه نبود is an existential negative: در زمانه = 'in that time/occasion' and نبود = past negative 'there was not'. The clause states absence of opportunity; the verb is past tense.
segments.s158-g20: برو here functions as the verbal base 'to go' (infinitival/lexical sense) and بر 'toward' marks motion; ز خوبی = 'because of/from goodness (i.e. on account of her beauty/kindness)' where ز is the poetic 'because of/of'. بهانه نبود is the main predication 'there was no excuse' (بهانه = 'excuse', نبود = 'there was not'). Together the phrase means 'there was no excuse to go because of her goodness.'
segments.s158-g21: بدو گفت گیو is inverted word order common in older Persian: بدو ('to her' = indirect-object prepositional clitic) + گفت (past 'said') + گیو (the speaker's name) — literally 'to her said Giv'. ای فریبنده ماه is a vocative: ای = 'O', فریبنده ماه = 'deceptive/alluring moon' (a poetic address calling someone beautiful).
segments.s158-g22: ترا is split here as ت + را (object clitic + object marker) and means 'you' as the object of the implied verb; سوی این بیشه = 'toward this grove'; چون بود راه؟ is an interrogative inversion meaning 'how was the way?' (چون = 'how', بود = past 'was', راه = 'path/road'). Together this asks 'By what road/way did you come to this grove?'
segments.s158-g23: چنین داد پاسخ که is a formula introducing reported speech: چنین = 'thus/so', داد (past 'gave') + پاسخ ('reply/answer') and که introduces the content clause. ما را is the object 'us' marked by را (object marker) and placed before the subject پدر for emphasis; syntactically this yields 'our father (پدر) [did something] to us (ما را)'.
segments.s158-g24: بزد is past 'he struck/beat' and دوش is the temporal adverb 'last night' (so 'پدر بزد دوش' = 'our father struck [us] last night'); بگذاشتم is 1st-person past 'I left' (ب- prefix + گذاشتم), and بوم و بر is an idiomatic pair 'home and land/realm' meaning 'my home and country'. Together the clause reports the reason for her departure.
segments.s158-g49: بدو گفت گیو is structured 'بدو' (to him) + گفت (said) + گیو (Giv) — the indirect-object prepositional pronoun بدو appears before the verb, as is common in older/poetic Persian; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here the lexical senses list given does not show the verbal sense). The following phrase ای سپهدار شاه is a vocative: ای addresses the addressee and سپهدار شاه is the title being addressed.
segments.s158-g50: نه begins a negative question ('will you not...? / will you...?'), با من برابر is a prepositional phrase 'equal with me', and بدی here functions as an older/poetic second‑person verbal form ('you will be/you stand') linking to the complement بی‌سپاه ('without an army'). Word order places the prepositional complement before the verbal predicate, typical of Persian.
segments.s158-g51: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that/that very'; طوس and نوذر are proper names (Tūs and Nowzar). The sequence بدان بستهید is a poetic compound: بـ (prefix 'to/at') + دان (here functioning within the compound, not the verb 'to know') + بستهید (past/compound verb 'you have bound' — بستهید is the past/perfect form meaning 'bound' with the -ید ending). The clause reads roughly 'That Tūs bound Nowzar to that.'
segments.s158-g52: کجا is an interrogative 'where'. پس of the colon the next clause gives the answer: پیش + اسپ من = 'in front of / before my steed' (پیش used as preposition with a possessor phrase 'اسپ من') and اینجا رسید is the verb phrase 'arrived here' (رسید past tense 'reached/arrived'). The verb comes at the end as in standard Persian clause order.
segments.s158-g53: بدو (to him) + گیو (Giv) + گفت (said) is the reporting main clause; این سخن 'this speech/remark' is the object being negated. خود is reflexive/emphatic 'yourself' or 'personally', and مگوی is the negative imperative 'do not say' (مگو with the negative imperative suffix -ی in older forms). The order 'object + negative imperative' is common in Persian speech.
segments.s158-g54: که introduces the quoted subordinate clause. من تاختم is a first‑person past of تاختن ('I charged/galloped'); پیش + نخچیرجوی is a prepositional phrase 'before/in front of the hunter' (پیش used as 'in the presence of / before'). The clause is a direct quotation of the boastful utterance.
segments.s158-g55: ز بهر is a fixed poetic phrase meaning 'for the sake of / because of' (ز here is a preposition fused with بهر). پرستنده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a worshipper/adorer' (the ـای ending marks indefiniteness), and گرمگوی is an adjective/participle 'warm-speaking / eager in praise' describing speech toward that person. The whole segment means roughly 'for the sake of a worshipper, (do not be) warm‑speaking.'
segments.s158-g56: نگردد is a negative/subjunctive/optative form of گشتن 'to become' (here 'should not become' / 'does not become'); جوانمرد is the subject 'the chivalrous/true man' and پرخاشجوی is the predicate adjective 'quarrelsome / aggressive'. Persian omits a copula, so نگردد links subject and predicate: 'a true man should not become quarrelsome.'
segments.s158-g57: سخن‌شان is a genitive/possessive construction (سخن + شان) = “their speech.” به تندی is an adverbial phrase (“in/with fierceness”), followed by بجایی رسید “reached a point” (literally “reached to a place/point”); رسید is simple past third-person singular. The phrase as a whole says their speech reached a sharp/pointed stage.
segments.s158-g58: که introduces a result clause (“that/so that”). این ماه را uses این ماه (literally “this moon,” here a kenning for a beautiful person) as definite object, marked by را. سر بباید برید contains بباید (modal “must/ought” in present or modal sense) plus برید (here the action verb “cut/behead” in subjunctive/infinitival sense); together: “it must be that the head of this moon be cut.”
segments.s158-g59: میانشان (“among them”) is میان + possessive شان. چو here functions as a temporal conjunction “when.” آن داوری شد is a verbal clause with شد (became/was made) meaning “that judgment was made/occurred,” and دراز qualifies the resulting state (“became long/protracted”).
segments.s158-g60: می‌انجی (here used as a noun from the stem in the lexicon) is the entity that “arises.” برآمد is past “arose/appeared.” یکی سرفراز is a postpositive descriptive phrase: یکی (“one/a certain person”) + سرفراز (an epithet, “proud/victorious/honored”) describing that person.
segments.s158-g61: که introduces a subordinate clause: این را (this as definite object) بر شاه ایران برید uses بر + شاه ایران (“to/toward the King of Iran”) and برید here functions as a subjunctive/imperative-like form from بردن “to take/bring” meaning “be brought/taken.” Grammatically this is a directive/result clause: “that this be brought to the Shah of Iran.”
segments.s158-g62: بدان is segmented here as ب (to/for) + دان, followed by کاو and دهد “give.” The clause functions as an instruction “give it to that one” (بدان = “to that [person]”), دهد = present/future “give.” هردو فرمان برید follows as a result: هردو “both,” فرمان “order/command,” and برید (here from بردن “to take/carry”) indicating they were commanded to take/carry it. (Some lexical items are archaic or split unusually in the manuscript; I render the syntactic roles rather than forcing modern forms.)
segments.s158-g63: نگشتند is past negative third-person plural of گشتن “to turn/turn away”: ن‑ negation + گشتند “they turned” = “they did not turn.” هردو “both” is the subject; ز گفتار اوی “from his speech” (ز = from; اوی = his) indicates they did not deviate from what he said.
segments.s158-g64: بر شاه ایران نهادند روی is an idiomatic sequence: بر شاه ایران “toward the King of Iran,” نهادند (past plural of نهادن) “they set/placed,” and روی “face.” Together نهادند روی = “they turned/ directed their face,” i.e. “they turned toward the Shah of Iran.” Word order places the target (بر شاه ایران) before the verb phrase.

sentences.s158-s1: Thus the mobad said that one day Tūs, at that place when the cock's cry arose, ...
sentences.s158-s2: He himself and Giv and Goudarz and a number of horsemen rode off joyfully from the king's gate.
sentences.s158-s3: To hunt the Guran's game, into the clamorous plain: aba-baz (falcons) and cheetahs, seekers of prey.
sentences.s158-s4: They gathered plentifully and cast/laid down (their spoil); they prepared forty days' worth of forage.
sentences.s158-s45: As for whatever other divine things ought to be, there was one red ruby not to be wasted.
sentences.s158-s33: When Kāvus saw the maid’s face, he laughed and bit his lip.
sentences.s158-s34: The king said thus to both spahbeds: 'The hardship of the road has been shortened for you.'
sentences.s158-s35: Let us spend the day on this tale — the day when the sun takes (seizes) the revolving, windless host.
sentences.s158-s36: Is it a stag, or is the beloved a gazelle?! Such a quarry is fitting for the chief at the threshold.
sentences.s158-s17: The footman said to him, "How did you come — unladen and without a guide?"
sentences.s158-s18: He answered thus: "My steed remained; because of weakness it cast me to the ground."
sentences.s158-s19: I had immeasurable gold and jewels; upon my head I had a single golden crown.
sentences.s158-s20: Thereupon they took it from my high brow; someone drew a sword from its scabbard and struck me.
sentences.s158-s13: A dark, intoxicated night came from the plain of Sur; just as, when from afar he saw me seething,
sentences.s158-s14: A certain man drew forth a bluish dagger; that same one wished to cut my head from my body.
sentences.s158-s15: They asked him, ‘O hero, of what lineage are you?’ — then he named the cypress-like (men) one by one.
sentences.s158-s16: He said to him: 'I myself am Garsivaz; I bring my victory to King Afrīdūn.'
sentences.s158-s41: I may anoint you with musk and gild you with gold; I will make you the foremost of the moon‑faced beauties.
sentences.s158-s42: He answered thus: 'I saw you; I chose you out of the rebels.'
sentences.s158-s43: The king sent the beloved into the inner chamber and commanded that she be seated at the appointed time.
sentences.s158-s44: They dressed her in yellow brocade, bedecked with ruby, turquoise, and lapis lazuli.
sentences.s158-s37: Khosrow said to her, "What is your lineage, that your face is like the face of a fairy?"
sentences.s158-s38: She answered him, "I am of Mam-Khatun; on my father's side I belong to Afrīdūn."
sentences.s158-s39: My ancestor is the commander Garsivaz; on that border his humble dwelling is the center.
sentences.s158-s40: He said to her, "If you desire this face, hair, and lineage, he will give all three to the wind."
sentences.s158-s21: When my father becomes awake, he will surely send a horseman; then I (will) cling to his skirt.
sentences.s158-s22: Even if the Arabs come, my mother will not wish that I leave this land and region.
sentences.s158-s23: The hearts of the champions softened toward him; the chief of Tūs, Nowzar, became shameless.
sentences.s158-s24: The Nowzari king said: 'I found (him); from Iran I hastened so swiftly.'
sentences.s158-s5: Near that spot a Turk was close by; his ground was dark from (or like) a donkey-stall.
sentences.s158-s6: A man came forth from the grove, from afar, toward the nearby border of Tūr's horsemen.
sentences.s158-s7: He rode on ahead with Tous; Giv then (came) with a few attendants playing the ney.
sentences.s158-s8: They went into that grove; both horsemen then turned about (returned) around that meadow.
sentences.s158-s9: Into the grove they found one with a beautiful face; with smiles on their lips, both hurried forward.
sentences.s158-s10: There was no opportunity in that time to meet him; there was no excuse to go away because of his goodness.
sentences.s158-s11: Giv said to her: 'O alluring moon, by what road did you come to this grove?'
sentences.s158-s12: Thus she answered: 'Last night our father struck us; I left my home and land.'
sentences.s158-s25: Giv said to him: O commander of the king, will you face me equally — without troops?
sentences.s158-s26: That same Tūs bound Nowzar to that — where? He arrived here before my steed.
sentences.s158-s27: Giv said to him: Do not say that yourself — 'I charged ahead of the hunter.'
sentences.s158-s28: Do not speak warmly for the sake of a worshipper; a true man does not become quarrelsome.
sentences.s158-s29: Their words grew so fierce that it came to the point that this moon’s head must be cut off.
sentences.s158-s30: When that judgment among them was prolonged, an (assembly/one from the gathering) rose up — a tall, proud man.
sentences.s158-s31: That this one be brought to the King of Iran; give it to that man — both were ordered to carry it.
sentences.s158-s32: Neither of the two turned away from his words; they set their faces toward the King of Iran.
